section.title: Peace with God
section.sectionRationale: With justification by faith established through Abraham, Paul now unfolds its glorious results. Because we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God, access into grace, and a sure hope of glory. That hope holds even in suffering, for suffering produces perseverance, character, and hope, and hope does not disappoint because God has poured His love into our hearts by the Spirit. The ground of it all is the death of Christ for us while we were still sinners and enemies, and the passage soars on an argument from the greater to the lesser: if God reconciled us by His Son's death when we were enemies, how much more will He save us by His Son's life now that we are His friends.
section.thesis: Having been justified by faith, believers enjoy peace with God, standing in grace and rejoicing in hope, a hope unshaken even by suffering because God's love is poured into their hearts; for while we were still sinners and enemies Christ died for us, and having been reconciled by His death we shall surely be saved by His life.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the fruits of justification, peace, access, and rejoicing hope even in suffering; the proof of God's love in Christ dying for the ungodly; and the how much more assurance that those reconciled by His death will be saved by His life.

units.label: Peace, access, and rejoicing hope
units.note: the fruits of justification, even in suffering

verses.text: Therefore, since we have been justified through faith, we have {{eirene|peace}} with God through our Lord Jesus Christ,
through whom we have gained access by faith into this grace in which we stand. And we rejoice in the {{elpis|hope}} of the glory of God.
Not only that, but we also rejoice in our sufferings, because we know that suffering produces perseverance;
And hope does not disappoint us, because God has poured out His {{agape|love}} into our hearts through the Holy Spirit, whom He has given us.

units.label: Christ died for the ungodly
units.note: God proves His love while we were still sinners

verses.text: For at just the right time, while we were still powerless, Christ died for the ungodly.
Very rarely will anyone die for a righteous man, though for a good man someone might possibly dare to die.
But God proves His love for us in this: While we were still sinners, Christ died for us.

units.label: Reconciled, saved by His life
units.note: the how much more of assurance

verses.text: Therefore, since we have now been justified by His blood, how much more shall we be saved from wrath through Him!
For if, when we were enemies of God, we were {{katallasso|reconciled}} to Him through the death of His Son, how much more, having been reconciled, shall we be saved through His life!
Not only that, but we also rejoice in God through our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom we have now received reconciliation.

eirene.gloss: Peace, in the deep biblical sense of wholeness and restored relationship, echoing the Hebrew shalom. This is not merely a feeling of calm but an objective reality: the war between the sinner and God is over. Having been justified, believers have peace with God, no longer under condemnation but welcomed as friends through our Lord Jesus Christ.

elpis.gloss: Hope, confident expectation of the glory of God. Christian hope is not uncertain wishing but settled assurance grounded in what God has already done in Christ. It holds firm even in suffering and does not disappoint, because it rests not on circumstances but on the love of God poured into the heart by the Spirit.

agape.gloss: Love, the self-giving love of God. It is proved not in words but in the death of Christ for us while we were still sinners and enemies. This love is poured out into the hearts of believers by the Holy Spirit, giving inward assurance that matches the outward demonstration at the cross.

katallasso.gloss: To reconcile, to change from enmity to friendship, to bring parties who were at war into peace. God reconciled us to Himself through the death of His Son while we were still His enemies. Reconciliation is His work, received as a gift, and it is the relational side of justification: the barrier is removed and the relationship restored.

basic.context: Paul has proved that sinners are justified by faith, using Abraham. Now he draws out what justification actually brings. The word therefore ties it all to what came before: because we are declared righteous, we have peace with God, we stand in His grace, and we look forward with joy to sharing His glory. Then Paul shows that this hope holds even under suffering, and grounds the whole thing in the love of God shown at the cross.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this as a chain of results flowing from justification, marked by therefore, and by two how much more arguments. Paul reasons from the greater to the lesser: if God did the hardest thing, dying for us while we were enemies, He will surely do the easier thing, saving us now that we are His friends. The logic is meant to give believers rock-solid assurance.
basic.originalLanguages: The first fruit is eirene, peace with God, the end of the war between the sinner and his Maker. Believers rejoice in elpis, hope, that holds even in suffering. That hope rests on agape, the love of God poured into the heart by the Spirit and proved at the cross. And the relationship itself is restored through katallasso, reconciliation, God changing enemies into friends through the death of His Son.
basic.typology: The peace and access Paul describes fulfill what the temple system only pictured. Under the old covenant access to God was guarded and limited; now, through Christ, believers have gained access by faith into grace and stand there freely. The reconciliation of enemies to God completes what every sacrifice for sin was reaching toward.
basic.christ: Everything here comes through our Lord Jesus Christ. He is the one through whom we have peace, through whom we gained access, and whose death for the ungodly proves God's love. Justified by His blood and reconciled by His death, we are saved by His life, His risen, interceding life. Christ crucified and risen is the ground of both our peace and our assurance.
basic.god: God is the one who justifies, who pours His love into believers' hearts by the Spirit, and who proves His love at the cross. The passage shows a God who takes the initiative toward enemies, reconciling them to Himself at the cost of His own Son. His love is not a reward for the lovely but a gift lavished on sinners.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 8 develops the same assurance: nothing can separate us from the love of God in Christ. Ephesians 2 says Christ is our peace, reconciling us to God through the cross. 2 Corinthians 5 declares that God was reconciling the world to Himself in Christ. James 1 echoes that trials produce perseverance.
basic.oneStory: From Eden the human race has been at war with God. The whole Bible moves toward peace restored. Here Paul announces that in Christ the war is over: enemies are reconciled, sinners are justified, and a sure hope of glory is given. The love that God has been showing all along is proved at the cross and poured into our hearts, carrying us all the way to glory.

context.literary: Romans 5:1-11 is a hinge in the letter, turning from the proof of justification by faith to its benefits and its security. It is bound together by the word therefore and by the two how much more arguments in verses 9 and 10. Verse 1 introduces peace, access, and hope; verses 3 to 5 handle suffering; verses 6 to 8 ground everything in the cross; verses 9 to 11 crown it with assurance and joy in God.
context.historical: Paul writes to believers in the imperial capital who knew the meaning of both peace and suffering. The Roman world prized the pax Romana, the peace enforced by empire; Paul speaks of a deeper peace, peace with God. His readers also faced real hardship, so his teaching that suffering produces perseverance and hope spoke directly to their situation.
context.canonical: This passage is one of the great New Testament statements of assurance and of the love of God. Its language of reconciliation joins 2 Corinthians 5 and Ephesians 2 as central texts on how the cross ends the enmity between God and sinners. It anchors the doctrine that those truly justified will certainly be saved to the end.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show believers the settled benefits of being justified by faith, peace, access, and hope, and to give them unshakable assurance that the God who reconciled them by His Son's death while they were enemies will certainly save them by His Son's life.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul is describing the actual standing and experience of all who are justified. The truths are prescriptive for the whole church: every believer has peace with God, may rejoice even in suffering, and can rest in the certainty of final salvation grounded in Christ.

debates.question: Should verse 1 read we have peace with God or let us have peace with God?

views.label: We have peace, the indicative
views.case: The context is a statement of fact about what justification secures, and most translations and the flow of the argument support the indicative: peace with God is a settled reality for the justified, not merely something to pursue.

views.label: Let us have peace, the exhortation
views.case: The manuscript evidence for the subjunctive is strong, and some read it as an exhortation to enjoy or hold on to the peace we have been given.

debates.bearing: Either way the peace is a gift already secured by justification; the historic reading takes it as fact, grounding assurance, while even the exhortation assumes the peace already exists to be enjoyed.

debates.question: What is the peace with God that believers have?

views.label: An objective, restored relationship
views.case: The primary meaning is that the war is over: God is no longer against the justified sinner but for him. This peace is a status, the end of enmity, grounded in the cross, echoing shalom as restored relationship.

views.label: Chiefly an inner tranquility
views.case: Some emphasize the subjective calm and rest that believers feel, the peace of God in the heart.

debates.bearing: The historic reading foregrounds the objective peace with God, the settled relationship; the inner peace of heart flows from it but is not the main point, so assurance rests on what God has done, not on how we feel.

study.originalLanguages: The passage opens with eirene, peace with God, the biblical shalom in which the war between the sinner and his Maker is ended and the relationship made whole. From that peace flows elpis, hope, the confident expectation of glory that does not disappoint even under suffering. The reason it does not disappoint is agape, the love of God poured out into the heart by the Holy Spirit and demonstrated at the cross while we were still sinners. And the deep work behind it all is katallasso, reconciliation: God changing enemies into friends through the death of His Son, a gift received, not an achievement earned.

typology.type: Access into the presence of God
typology.fulfillment: Fulfills the limited access of the temple; through Christ believers now stand freely in grace, the way to God opened by His blood.
Completes what the sin offerings pictured, as God reconciles sinners to Himself once for all through the death of His Son.
The wholeness longed for since Eden, secured in Christ our peace, Ephesians 2:14-16.

study.christ: Christ is the channel of every blessing in the passage. Through our Lord Jesus Christ we have peace; through Him we gained access into grace; through His death for the ungodly God proves His love; and through His risen life we are kept and finally saved. Justified by His blood and reconciled by His death, believers are secured by His living intercession. The cross is the demonstration of divine love, and the resurrection is the guarantee of complete salvation, so Christ crucified and risen holds our assurance from beginning to end.
study.god: The God of this passage takes the initiative toward those who were powerless, ungodly, sinners, and enemies. He does not wait for us to reconcile ourselves but reconciles us through the death of His Son, and pours His love into our hearts by the Spirit. His love is proved, not merely declared, at the cross, and His character guarantees that what He began He will complete. This is a God whose grace is the sure foundation of hope.

crossReferences.note: The same how much more assurance: if God gave His Son, nothing can separate us from His love.
Christ is our peace, reconciling us to God through the cross and giving access to the Father.
God reconciling the world to Himself in Christ and giving the ministry of reconciliation.
The testing of faith produces perseverance, echoing suffering that produces character and hope.

study.oneStory: Since Eden humanity has stood at odds with God, hiding, guilty, and afraid. The whole Bible moves toward reconciliation. Here Paul announces that in Christ the war is over: the justified have peace with God, stand in grace, and rejoice in the hope of glory. The love God had been unfolding all along is proved at the cross, where Christ died for enemies, and poured into believing hearts by the Spirit, carrying them safely home.

quiz.q: What is the first result of being justified by faith, according to verse 1?

options.text: We have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ.
options.note: Justification ends the enmity; the war between the sinner and God is over.

options.text: We are given a peaceful, calm feeling inside.
options.note: Inner peace may follow, but the point is objective peace with God, a restored relationship.

options.text: We are freed from all suffering in this life.
options.note: Paul says we still suffer, yet rejoice; peace with God is not the removal of hardship.

options.text: We earn a right standing by keeping the peace.
options.note: Peace is the fruit of justification by faith, not something we earn.

quiz.q: Why can believers rejoice even in their sufferings?

options.text: Because suffering produces perseverance, character, and hope that does not disappoint.
options.note: God uses suffering to forge a hope grounded in His love poured into our hearts.

options.text: Because suffering earns them extra merit before God.
options.note: Suffering forms character; it does not earn merit or acceptance.

options.text: Because they should pretend that pain does not hurt.
options.note: Paul is not denying the pain but pointing to what God works through it.

options.text: Because suffering guarantees an easier life to come.
options.note: Suffering does look toward a future hope, but the fruit is perseverance, character, and hope, not the promise of an easy life.

quiz.q: How does God prove His love for us, according to verse 8?

options.text: While we were still sinners, Christ died for us.
options.note: God's love is demonstrated at the cross, given to the undeserving.

options.text: By waiting until we had made ourselves worthy.
options.note: Christ died while we were still sinners, powerless, and ungodly.

options.text: By rewarding those who first loved Him.
options.note: The love is one-way grace toward enemies, not a reward for our love.

options.text: Mainly by giving us comfortable lives.
options.note: God is good to us, but the proof of His love is the death of Christ, not favorable circumstances.

quiz.q: What is the how much more argument in verses 9 and 10?

options.text: If God reconciled us by His Son's death when we were enemies, He will surely save us by His life.
options.note: Paul reasons from the harder act to the easier, grounding assurance.

options.text: If we try harder, God will love us even more.
options.note: The argument rests on Christ's finished work, not our effort.

options.text: That salvation is now uncertain and depends on us.
options.note: The point is the opposite: our salvation is made certain by Christ.

options.text: That reconciliation matters more than final salvation.
options.note: Both are precious, but Paul's point is that they belong together: reconciliation guarantees final salvation.

quiz.q: What does it mean that we have received reconciliation?

options.text: God has changed us from enemies into friends through the death of His Son.
options.note: Reconciliation is God's work, received as a gift, ending the enmity.

options.text: We have reconciled ourselves to God by our repentance.
options.note: Repentance is real, but reconciliation is accomplished by God through Christ's death and then received.

options.text: God and humanity met each other halfway.
options.note: God took the whole initiative toward enemies; it was not a mutual bargain.

options.text: It refers only to peace between people, not with God.
options.note: Paul speaks of being reconciled to God Himself through His Son.

questions: Paul says the justified have peace with God, a settled end to the war. How would living in the certainty of that peace, rather than fearing God's rejection, change your daily walk with Him?
Believers rejoice in the hope of glory even in suffering. Where is God using a present hardship to produce perseverance, character, and hope in you?
God proves His love in that Christ died for us while we were still sinners. How does knowing His love was given to you as an enemy, not a reward for your goodness, move your heart?
The Spirit pours God's love into our hearts. How have you known that inward assurance of being loved, and how might you ask God for a deeper sense of it?
If we were reconciled by Christ's death, how much more shall we be saved by His life. How does this how much more logic steady your assurance when you doubt your salvation?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote records a variant, let us have peace, reflecting strong manuscript support for the subjunctive. The BSB text reads we have peace, taking it as a statement of fact about the standing justification secures; either way the peace is a gift already given.
Rejoice renders a verb the BSB footnote notes can also be exult, used again in verses 3 and 11. It expresses confident, glad boasting in what God has done, not in ourselves.

Reconciled renders katallasso, to change from enmity to friendship. The verse stresses that reconciliation was accomplished through the death of God's Son while we were enemies, so that final salvation through His risen life is assured.
